Urban Planning · Hebron Old City · Palestine · 15 Slides

Reviving
Al-Hisbeh

How can we recreate the spatial functionality of the Hisbeh market within larger rehabilitation plans for Hebron's old city?

إحياء الحسبة — سوق الخضار التاريخي · مدينة الخليل القديمة

January 2025 · Field Research HRC · UNESCO WHC · OBL/QUE Vol.6
Scroll
02 — Why This Matters

A Space That Shaped Me

Growing up in Hebron, the old city was not a relic — it was alive. The Hisbeh was the smell of Wednesday mornings: coriander, earth, the call of vendors from the Yatta road.

My connection to this project is personal before it is academic. The building is still standing. The stone arches of Khan al-Shaheen are still there. What was destroyed was not the fabric — it was the life that moved through it.

As OBL/QUE editor Rana Abughannam writes: "Heritage transcends buildings, landmarks, or monuments; it is a complex weave of material and immaterial culture that anchors Palestinians to their homeland." This project documents that weave before it is fully severed.

"I chose to study a space that is not technically destroyed — but whose culture and old use has been destroyed."
OBL/QUE — Critical Conservation, Vol. 6 (2025)

"Turath is not a single Outstanding Universal Value, but an expansive associated ecology of connections, links, kinships, and fundamental groundings. Turath is resistance, it is resilience, and it is the manifestation of Palestinian sumud."

— Rana Abughannam, "Voices from Palestine" · OBL/QUE Vol. 6, 2025

215,000
Palestinian residents of Hebron
850
Israeli settlers in the old city core whose presence disrupts all
200+
Shops closed by Israeli Military Order since 2000
1/3
Of Palestinian properties in old city now abandoned (OCHA, 2019)
2017
UNESCO listed Hebron Old City as World Heritage — immediately "in danger"
1998
Aga Khan Award for Architecture — HRC rehabilitation efforts, Hebron

Sources: Visualizing Palestine · HRC · MERIP · OBL/QUE Vol.6 · OCHA 2019

03 — The Problem · The Logic

The Architecture of Erasure

The Hisbeh's closure was not arbitrary. It followed a precise spatial logic — the dismantling of Palestinian commercial life in H2 Hebron under Israeli military control after the 1997 Hebron Protocol.

01
Settler Proximity Trigger

The Hisbeh sits adjacent to the settler zone. Under Israeli policy, Palestinian commercial activity near settlements is classified as a security threat. Closure follows settlement presence — not the reverse. The 1994 massacre at the Ibrahimi Mosque by settler Baruch Goldstein triggered the first wave of curfews and closures.

02
Military Order 3 — Blanket Closure

Over 200 shops in the surrounding old city were shuttered by military orders. The wholesale and vegetable markets were targeted first — severing the supply chain that connected the old city to the Hebron Governorate's agricultural hinterland. Al-Shuhada Street has been closed to Palestinians since 1997.

03
Political Entropy

Research published in ScienceDirect (2024) identifies Israel's strategy as "political entropy" — using time and prohibition of renovation to let Palestinian assets decay slowly. Buildings are left empty, declared dangerous, then seized. The Hisbeh followed this trajectory: closed 2000, decayed, formally annexed after October 2023.

04
Demographic Pressure

As commerce dies, residents leave. Without residents, more shops shutter. The HRC estimates at least one-third of Palestinian properties in the old city are now abandoned — creating the justification for further settler expansion. The cycle is by design. UNESCO's 2024 SOC report documents ongoing demolition of commercial stores in Al-Hisbeh market.

Sources: ScienceDirect "Politics of Time" (2024) · OCHA · B'Tselem · UNESCO SOC 2024 · Visualizing Palestine

04 — Consequences

How Closure Altered Daily Life

The Hisbeh was not just a market. It was the urban artery — the point where farmers from Yatta and Dura met glassblowers from Souq al-Qazazin and families from the old city neighborhoods. Its closure severed all of these connections simultaneously.

  • — Supply chain collapse. Farmers from across the governorate lost their primary market. Traders resorted to "back-to-back" transport — moving goods between vehicles at military gates — just to stock their shops.
  • — Social rupture. The daily gathering function disappeared. The Hisbeh served as the rare open breathing space in the dense urban fabric — its closure eliminated this shared civic ground.
  • — Abandonment cascade. Without commerce, residents left. Al-Shuhada Street — once Hebron's commercial spine — became inaccessible to Palestinians from 1997 onward.
  • — Physical deterioration. With Palestinians barred from repairing H2 properties, the built fabric decays — creating the legal pretext for further seizures. The Hisbeh building itself was formally annexed after October 2023.
1994
Ibrahimi Mosque Massacre
Settler Baruch Goldstein kills 29 Palestinians during Ramadan prayer. Curfews imposed on Palestinians — not settlers. First wave of shop closures begins. The old city's commercial activity starts its long decline.
1997
Hebron Protocol — Al-Shuhada Closed
City divided H1/H2. Al-Shuhada Street — formerly the main commercial artery — closed to Palestinians, accessible exclusively to settlers. The old city is effectively cut off from the wider city.
2000
Al-Hisbeh Closed by Military Order
Second Intifada triggers Military Order 3. Al-Hisbeh market forcibly closed. 200+ surrounding shops shuttered. The wholesale vegetable market relocates to H1 — inaccessible to old city residents. The social and economic anchor of the old city is severed.
2017–2018
HRC Rehabilitation — Khan al-Shaheen
UNESCO lists Hebron Old City as World Heritage and simultaneously places it "in danger." Khan al-Shaheen rehabilitated with UNESCO/SIDA funding. HRC wins Aga Khan Award. Adjacent housing restored and repopulated.
2023–Present
Escalation — Building Seized
Post-October 7 escalation. Israeli forces halt HRC renovation works. Hisbeh building formally seized. New settlement outposts announced. UNESCO SOC 2024 documents demolition of commercial stores and destruction of historic bus station arches.
05 — The Need

Why Palestinians Need This Center

In Palestinian culture, the market — the souq — is not commercial infrastructure. It is the architecture of public life itself. In Arabic, heritage is turath (تراث) — inherited connection to land, neighbours, songs, food, and community.

Public Space as Identity

Al-Khalil (Hebron) is the West Bank's largest trade hub, historically known for glass-blowing, ceramics, and agricultural produce. The old city's souqs were not just where things were sold — they were where Hebronite identity was performed, daily, collectively. The Hisbeh was the most egalitarian: rural women, wholesale traders, children, families — all present.

Collapse of Public / Private Boundaries

Under military occupation, public Palestinian space is eliminated while private Palestinian space is invaded. Checkpoints transform every movement into a negotiation with armed authority. The result is "ontological insecurity" — Palestinians in H2 cannot know day to day which paths will be open. Reviving a market is an act of reclaiming predictable public ground.

Economic Survival

Commerce in the old city has come to near-halt. Traders resort to "back-to-back" transport — moving goods between vehicles at military gates. Reviving a weekly market reconnects the agricultural economy of the governorate to the craft economy of the old city — the link that made Hebron regionally significant for centuries.

Critical Conservation as Resistance

OBL/QUE (Vol. 6, 2025) frames conservation in Palestine as sumud — steadfastness. Nuha Dandis's essay "Preserving Living Heritage" documents the HRC's continual efforts to rehabilitate Hebron, "one of the most militarized cities in the West Bank," as a form of resistance. This project extends that framework to the Hisbeh's functional revival.

OBL/QUE Vol. 6 · Rana Abughannam, 2025

"Conservation architects have the opportunity and responsibility to renegotiate history and power relationships through design. To conserve means to question, revise, and subvert dominant versions of the past rather than its passive and complicit perpetuation."

— OBL/QUE: Critical Conservation, ISSN 2474-6347

06 — Research Question

Central Question

How can we recreate the spatial functionality of the Hisbeh market within larger rehabilitation plans for Hebron's old city?

01
What Are We Studying?

The physical and social geography of the Hisbeh closure — the spatial logic that made this market a target, and what its absence has produced in the surrounding urban fabric of Hebron's old city.

02
What Are We Learning?

How settler-colonial spatial strategies operate at the scale of a single market building — and what precedents exist for functional revival without physical restoration of the seized building.

03
What Are We Discovering?

That the culture of the Hisbeh — the social network, the rhythm of market day, the regional food supply chain — can be reconstituted in adjacent accessible space using existing rehabilitated infrastructure.

07 — Spatial Context · Qazazin Quarter

Walking Through Souq al-Qazazin

Before examining data, experience the space. This walkthrough of the Qazazin quarter shows the current state of the adjacent market corridor — the zone where the proposed intervention would operate.

The architecture is intact. The stone vaulting of the covered souq, the shop fronts, the scale of the lanes. What is missing is not the physical fabric — it is the people, the trade, the noise. This is the argument of the project.

"The buildings are here. The corridor exists. The problem is a functional and cultural erasure — and therefore the solution is a functional and cultural revival."
  • → Note the proximity to the Hisbeh back entrance
  • → Note the stone vaulting — structurally intact
  • → Note the abandoned shops — all structurally sound
  • → Note the absence of foot traffic

Walkthrough of Souq al-Qazazin — adjacent to Al-Hisbeh, Hebron Old City

08 — Methodology

Data Collection + Analysis

🎙

Oral History Interviews

Semi-structured interviews with former Hisbeh traders, old city residents, and HRC staff. Questions focused on use patterns, social function, memory, and resilience strategies.

Qualitative
🗺

GIS Spatial Mapping

Multi-layer GIS analysis: commercial status (open/partial/military closure), building occupancy, checkpoint locations, and settler outpost boundaries. Mapped on Palestine 1923 Grid.

Spatial Analysis
📸

Field Documentation

Physical survey of the Hisbeh site, Khan al-Shaheen corridor, Souq al-Qazazin, and Hammam al-Na'im. Photography of current conditions and access routes.

Field Research
📚

Archival Sources

UNESCO WHC documentation, Visualizing Palestine datasets, HRC project archives, B'Tselem, OCHA, OBL/QUE Vol. 6 (Critical Conservation), peer-reviewed spatial analysis (ScienceDirect, 2024).

Secondary Research
🏛

Comparative Precedents

Functional market revival in occupied or post-conflict heritage zones — cases where cultural function was restored without physical recovery of the original building.

Comparative
🔄

Iterative Co-Design

Proposal refined in dialogue with HRC staff and remaining traders in adjacent souqs. Market schedule, vendor structure, and hostel concept developed participatorily.

Participatory
09 — Main Findings · Oral History

What People Remember

Seven structured interview questions. The answers consistently reveal the same layers: social function, regional connection, sensory memory, and ongoing resilience.

How did people use the Hisbeh?
Farmers and agricultural workers came from across the governorate — from Yatta, Dura, Dhahiriya — to sell produce. The site functioned as an open market beyond the building itself: vegetables, secondhand goods (bale), wholesale and retail trade. Frequented by rural women selling seasonal goods, families, and merchants.
What social role did it play beyond commerce?
"The sounds of vendors calling out their goods created a distinctive and vibrant atmosphere for the town." The Hisbeh served as a daily meeting hub and the rare open breathing space in the densely packed old city. Its courtyards allowed goods to be displayed where narrow lanes could not.
How did traders respond to closures?
Resilience and steadfastness (sumud). Shop owners maintained presence in surrounding markets. Many developed the back-to-back system — moving goods between vehicles at military gates — to keep supply chains alive. The HRC documented this as everyday resistance.
What did the HRC achieve in this area?
Restoration of all adjacent housing units and their repopulation. Infrastructure rehabilitation throughout. Restoration of Hammam al-Na'im. Khan al-Shaheen market rehabilitated in 2018 (UNESCO/SIDA). Opening of institutional offices — maintaining Palestinian institutional presence in H2.
10 — Main Findings · Counter-Logic

Countering the Colonial Logic

Each mechanism of erasure in the problem has a counter-finding — evidence that Palestinian spatial culture survived the closure and can be revived.

Colonial Logic (Problem)Counter-Finding (Resilience)
Closure eliminates the vegetable supply chain from the governorate
Traders maintained informal supply routes; regional vendor networks are intact and reactivatable
Physical decay of the Hisbeh building enables seizure
The intervention operates in the corridor between Khan al-Shaheen and Qazazin — rehabilitated, accessible, outside the seized building
Demographic pressure empties the old city
HRC repopulated adjacent housing; 33,000 Palestinians still live in H2 — the market's potential user base exists
Preventing renovation enables political entropy and decay
Khan al-Shaheen (2018) and Hammam al-Na'im (restored) provide ready infrastructure for hostel + market concept
Seizure of the Hisbeh building makes restoration impossible
The function of the Hisbeh does not require its building — it requires a weekly rhythmic gathering, accessible public space, and a regional vendor network
OBL/QUE Vol. 6 · Nuha Dandis, "Preserving Living Heritage" (2025)

"Looking at Hebron, one of the most militarized cities in the West Bank — the HRC's continual efforts to preserve and rehabilitate the old city exemplify how built heritage rehabilitation can serve as a tool of resistance and a practice of sumud."

11 — Focus · Spatial Details

The Spatial Evidence

Khan al-Shaheen

Rehabilitated 2018. Its rear courtyard directly abuts the proposed market zone. The caravanserai typology is historically the correct anchor for a regional market — it was designed to serve exactly this function.

Hisbeh Back Entrance Corridor

The passage between Khan al-Shaheen and Souq al-Qazazin remains physically accessible to Palestinians. It opens into a wider node — sufficient for weekly market setup without requiring entry into the seized building.

Hammam al-Na'im

The adjacent restored bathhouse and old guest house are structurally sound. The guest house — historically used by visiting merchants — is available for adaptive reuse as the proposed overnight hostel.

Souq al-Qazazin

Stone vaulting intact. Shop fronts structurally sound. The 2024 UNESCO SOC report notes the historic bus station arches were destroyed — making the Qazazin souq the remaining covered route from the Hisbeh site to the Ibrahimi Mosque.

Israeli violations in the core zone of Hebron Old Town. Blue: Al-Hisbeh Market. Red: Osama School (seized). Green: Al-Ibrahimi Mosque. Source: HRC / Hebron Rehabilitation Committee

12 — Focus · Interactive & Reconstructed Maps

Interactive Layer Maps

Legend
Overview of Hebron Old City core. Click markers for details. Use tabs to switch between map layers and your own GIS maps.
13 — Proposal

Three Moves, One Strategy

The proposal operates entirely within currently accessible Palestinian space — no seizure reversal required.

01

Weekly Produce Market

A rotating weekly market at the back entrance of the Hisbeh site — the passage between Khan al-Shaheen and the end of Souq al-Qazazin. Vendors from across the Hebron Governorate attend. The rhythm of a fixed market day re-establishes the public gathering function without touching the seized building.

02

Merchants' Hostel

The old guest house near Hammam al-Na'im is converted to overnight accommodation for out-of-city traders. This removes the barrier preventing farmers from Yatta or Dhahiriya from spending market day in the old city — restoring the regional market function the Hisbeh historically provided.

03

Souq al-Qazazin Activation

Coordinated activation of Qazazin with the market creates a continuous active zone from Khan al-Shaheen through the Hisbeh back entrance to the Ibrahimi Mosque — restoring the historic commercial spine of the old city.

Commercial activity in the Souq, Thallatha al-Qadim and Al-Shohadaa Street zone. Green = Open. Orange = Partially operating. Red = Voluntarily closed. Blue = Closed by Military Order. Purple = Old Thallatha Street shops. Source: HRC GIS

14 — Contribution

What This Research Contributes

📐

To Urban Planning Practice

A spatial methodology for functional revival within occupied or restricted heritage zones where physical restoration of the original building is impossible. The project models how urban function can be re-seeded in adjacent accessible space — applicable beyond Hebron.

📖

To Heritage Scholarship

A case study for UNESCO's "heritage in danger" framework and the OBL/QUE Critical Conservation paradigm — demonstrating that turath (intangible cultural heritage: market culture, vendor networks, regional supply rhythms) can be the intervention target, not just physical fabric.

🗺

To GIS Documentation

The multi-layer map produced fills a gap in publicly available spatial documentation of the Hisbeh closure — contributing to the Visualizing Palestine and HRC archives. All maps produced on the Palestine 1923 Grid for compatibility with existing HRC datasets.

✊

To Palestinian Civil Society

A concrete, implementable proposal — not contingent on political resolution — that the HRC, municipal actors, and regional vendors can begin working toward now. Aligned with the HRC's mission established by Presidential Decree, August 12, 1996: reviving the old city while preserving its cultural heritage.

"Through preserving historical buildings, renovating community spaces, and protecting cultural landmarks, Palestinians assert their continuous presence on the land, grounding themselves and future generations in it." — Rana Abughannam, OBL/QUE Vol. 6 (2025)

Summary · Slide 15 of 15

The building is seized.
The culture is not.

Al-Hisbeh was not destroyed. It was closed, then decayed, then seized — by design. The logic of political entropy sought to make emptiness permanent. This project argues that the vendor networks, the regional supply rhythms, the social grammar of a market day all survive — waiting for a container. Khan al-Shaheen is that container. The Qazazin corridor is that container. The memory of every person who remembers a Wednesday morning at the Hisbeh is that container.

  • Field interviews, January 2025
Central Question

How can we recreate the spatial functionality of the Hisbeh market within larger rehabilitation plans for Hebron's old city?

By operating within the accessible corridor — reviving the vendor network, installing the missing infrastructure (weekly market, hostel, activated souq) — without waiting for political resolution of the building's seizure. Heritage as sumud.
